title: Hematology & Oncology — USMLE Step 1
description: Anemias, leukemias, lymphomas, coagulation disorders, sickle cell, thalassemia, and key hematology pharmacology.
subject: medicine

cards.back: ↓ MCV (microcytic), ↓ serum iron, ↑ TIBC, ↓ ferritin (best indicator of stores), ↓ transferrin saturation

cards.front: Anemia of chronic disease: lab findings
cards.back: Microcytic or normocytic. ↓ serum iron, ↓ TIBC (opposite of iron deficiency), ↑ ferritin (acute phase reactant), ↓ transferrin saturation
Subacute combined degeneration — demyelination of dorsal columns (vibration/proprioception loss) + lateral corticospinal tracts (UMN signs)

cards.front: Vitamin B12 vs folate deficiency: key distinction
cards.back: Both cause megaloblastic anemia (hypersegmented neutrophils, macrocytic). B12 deficiency also causes neurological symptoms (folate does NOT). B12: ↑ MMA and homocysteine. Folate: ↑ homocysteine only
Defect in spectrin/ankyrin → spherocytes → extravascular hemolysis. Osmotic fragility test positive. Treatment: splenectomy

cards.front: G6PD deficiency: triggers and blood smear
cards.back: X-linked recessive. Triggers: oxidative stress (infection, fava beans, primaquine, dapsone). Blood smear: Heinz bodies (denatured Hb), bite cells

cards.front: Sickle cell disease: mutation and crisis types
cards.back: HbS = β-globin Glu→Val (E6V). Vasoocclusive (pain) crisis, splenic sequestration, aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), acute chest syndrome
Autosplenectomy (↑ encapsulated organism infections), avascular necrosis (femoral head), osteomyelitis (Salmonella), renal papillary necrosis, stroke, retinopathy
1 gene: silent carrier. 2 genes: trait (mild microcytic anemia). 3 genes: HbH disease (tetramers of β-chains). 4 genes: hydrops fetalis (tetramers of γ-chains) — fatal in utero
Chronic transfusions (causes iron overload → chelation with deferoxamine or deferasirox). Bone marrow transplant is curative

cards.front: Hemolytic anemia: direct Coombs vs indirect Coombs
cards.back: Direct (DAT): detects antibodies bound to RBCs (autoimmune hemolysis). Indirect: detects free antibodies in serum (blood bank crossmatching)

cards.front: Warm vs cold autoimmune hemolytic anemia
cards.back: Warm (IgG, 37°C): SLE, CLL, methyldopa, penicillin. Cold (IgM, <37°C): Mycoplasma, EBV, CLL. Cold agglutinin disease: Raynaud-like, acrocyanosis
↑ PT, ↑ PTT, ↓ platelets, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer, ↑ fibrin split products. Schistocytes on smear. Causes: sepsis, trauma, obstetric complications, malignancy
Activates antithrombin III → inhibits factors IIa and Xa (unfractionated heparin). Reversal: protamine sulfate
Inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis (II, VII, IX, X + protein C, S). Reversal: vitamin K (FFP for immediate reversal)
IgG antibodies against heparin-PF4 complex → platelet activation → thrombosis (HITT). Onset: 5–10 days after heparin. Stop all heparin; use direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban, bivalirudin)
DDAVP (desmopressin) — releases vWF from endothelial Weibel-Palade bodies. Type 3 and platelet-type vWD: vWF concentrate

cards.front: Hemophilia A vs B: deficient factors and treatment
cards.back: Hemophilia A: factor VIII deficiency. Hemophilia B: factor IX deficiency. X-linked recessive. Hemarthroses are classic. Treatment: recombinant factor VIII or IX

cards.front: Coagulation cascade: intrinsic vs extrinsic pathway tests
cards.back: Intrinsic (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII): measured by PTT. Extrinsic (factor VII): measured by PT/INR. Common pathway (X, V, II, I): both tests elevated
Factors II, VII, IX, X + Protein C and Protein S (all need vitamin K for γ-carboxylation)

cards.front: Chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML): mutation and treatment
cards.back: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) → BCR-ABL fusion → constitutive tyrosine kinase. Treatment: imatinib (Gleevec, TKI). Blast crisis → accelerated phase management

cards.front: Acute myelogenous leukemia (AML): subtype associated with DIC
cards.back: AML-M3 (APL — acute promyelocytic leukemia): t(15;17) → PML-RARα fusion. Treatment: ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) + arsenic trioxide

cards.front: ALL: most common leukemia in children
cards.back: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia. TdT positive (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase). Burkitt-type ALL: t(8;14), c-myc. CNS prophylaxis required
Most common leukemia in adults (> 60 yr). CD5+ B cells. Smudge cells on smear. Autoimmune hemolytic anemia. Richter transformation (→ diffuse large B-cell lymphoma)

cards.front: Hairy cell leukemia: features and treatment
cards.back: Rare B-cell malignancy. TRAP (tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase) positive. Pancytopenia, massive splenomegaly. Treatment: cladribine (2-CdA)
Classic RS cell: CD15+, CD30+. Bimodal age distribution (20s and 60s). Better prognosis than NHL
Nodular sclerosis (most common in young adults). Classic Reed-Sternberg cells in lacunar variants

cards.front: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma: most common subtype in adults
cards.back: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). Aggressive but potentially curable with R-CHOP (rituximab + cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin + vincristine + prednisone)
t(8;14) → c-myc overexpression. EBV associated (African form). 'Starry sky' appearance (macrophages eating apoptotic cells). Highly aggressive
Calcium ↑, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions (punched-out lytic lesions). Also: M-protein on SPEP, Bence-Jones proteins in urine, bone marrow > 10% plasma cells

cards.front: Multiple myeloma: most common cause of death
cards.back: Infections (due to immunosuppression and hypogammaglobulinemia). Renal failure is second
JAK2 V617F mutation (>95%). ↑ RBC mass + ↑ WBC + ↑ platelets. Aquagenic pruritus (after hot shower), splenomegaly, Budd-Chiari syndrome. Treatment: phlebotomy + hydroxyurea
Platelets > 450k, JAK2/CALR/MPL mutations. Can paradoxically cause thrombosis AND bleeding. Treatment: aspirin; hydroxyurea if high risk

cards.front: Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP): mechanism and treatment
cards.back: IgG antibodies against platelet glycoproteins (GPIIb/IIIa, GPIb). Treatment: prednisone (first-line), IVIG, splenectomy, romiplostim/eltrombopag (TPO agonists)
TTP: ADAMTS13 deficiency, more neurological symptoms, predominantly adults. HUS: primarily pediatric, E. coli O157:H7, more renal involvement

cards.front: Platelet function: primary vs secondary hemostasis
cards.back: Primary hemostasis: platelet plug (vWF bridges platelets to damaged endothelium via GPIb; ADP activates via P2Y12 receptor). Secondary: coagulation cascade (fibrin clot formation)

cards.front: ADP P2Y12 receptor inhibitors (mechanism and drug examples)
cards.back: Clopidogrel, prasugrel, ticagrelor — block ADP-mediated platelet activation. Clopidogrel/prasugrel: irreversible. Ticagrelor: reversible
Destruction of pluripotent stem cells → pancytopenia. Causes: autoimmune (most common), drugs (chloramphenicol, benzene), viruses. Treatment: immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) or allogenic BMT
Autosomal recessive DNA repair defect. Short stature, thumb/radial abnormalities, café au lait spots, pancytopenia, high cancer risk (AML, SCC)
Dysplastic bone marrow with cytopenias. Ring sideroblasts (iron in mitochondria around nucleus), hypersegmented neutrophils, macro-ovalocytes. Risk of transformation to AML
Hemorrhagic cystitis (acrolein metabolite). Prevent with mesna (uroprotectant) and aggressive hydration. Also causes SIADH, myelosuppression, bladder cancer (long-term)
Dilated cardiomyopathy (dose-dependent), myelosuppression, alopecia. Monitor with echocardiogram. Cardioprotection with dexrazoxane
Pulmonary fibrosis (dose-limiting toxicity). Skin changes (Raynaud, hyperpigmentation). Minimal myelosuppression
Peripheral neuropathy (foot drop), SIADH. Minimal myelosuppression (contrast with vinblastine — predominantly myelosuppressive)
Nephrotoxicity (aggressive hydration + amifostine for prevention), ototoxicity (high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss), peripheral neuropathy, severe nausea/vomiting
↑ potassium, ↑ phosphate, ↑ uric acid, ↓ calcium, ↑ creatinine. Prevention: allopurinol (or rasburicase), aggressive IV hydration, monitor electrolytes

cards.front: DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation): pathophysiology, labs, treatment
cards.back: Widespread thrombin activation → simultaneous clotting + bleeding. Labs: ↑PT, ↑aPTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer, ↓platelets, schistocytes. Causes: sepsis, trauma, obstetric emergencies (placental abruption), AML-M3 (ATRA reverses). Treatment: treat underlying cause; FFP for clotting factors; platelets if <50k; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100.

cards.front: Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML): molecular target and treatment
cards.back: BCR-ABL fusion gene (Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)) → constitutively active tyrosine kinase. Presents in chronic phase with ↑WBC, basophilia, splenomegaly; blasts <10%. Treatment: imatinib (1st gen TKI), dasatinib/nilotinib (2nd gen). Resistance: T315I mutation → use ponatinib. Blast crisis: ≥20% blasts (AML or ALL phenotype). Monitor: PCR for BCR-ABL.

cards.front: Sickle cell disease: complications and preventive therapies
cards.back: HbS: valine→glutamate substitution. Complications: vaso-occlusive pain crisis, acute chest syndrome (most common cause of death), stroke, splenic sequestration, aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), priapism, avascular necrosis, infections (Strep pneumo, Salmonella osteomyelitis). Prevention: hydroxyurea (↑HbF), penicillin prophylaxis, pneumococcal vaccine. Exchange transfusion: stroke, ACS.

cards.front: Transfusion reactions: acute hemolytic vs febrile non-hemolytic vs TRALI
cards.back: Acute hemolytic: ABO mismatch → intravascular hemolysis → fever, chills, hypotension, flank pain, hemoglobinuria. Most dangerous, can be fatal. Febrile non-hemolytic: recipient antibodies against donor WBC antigens → fever/chills without hemolysis; most common reaction. TRALI (transfusion-related acute lung injury): donor anti-HLA antibodies → noncardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours; leading cause of transfusion death.
